expectations.text: Diagnoses the root cause as the DB connection being held across the outbound Stripe HTTP call (transaction scope too wide), not just a missing close
Recommends splitting into two short transactions (load before HTTP call, save after), with the Stripe call OUTSIDE the @Transactional boundary; shows code
Recommends enabling HikariCP `leak-detection-threshold` (typically 30000ms or similar) so future leaks log a stack trace of the holder
Explicitly warns against just raising maximumPoolSize as 'the fix' — calls it masking the leak
Verification plan re-uses pg_stat_activity to confirm no sessions stuck idle in transaction post-fix
References HikariCP MBean attribute(s) such as ActiveConnections, ThreadsAwaitingConnection for live diagnosis or verification
Mentions Postgres-side guardrail (idle_in_transaction_session_timeout and/or statement_timeout) as a server-side circuit breaker
Notes that splitting a transaction around an external API call introduces a crash window and recommends an idempotency key (and/or reconciliation job)
Diagnoses this as a lifecycle leak (cleanup on cancel, not steady-state) tied to job restart count, not a per-record leak
Identifies the missing or insufficient ManagedChannel.shutdown() / .awaitTermination() call in RichAsyncFunction.close() as the proximate cause
Provides a corrected close() body that shuts down the ManagedChannel (with awaitTermination + shutdownNow fallback)
Recommends an idempotent close() (volatile closed flag) because Flink can call close() multiple times
Mentions overriding RichAsyncFunction.timeout() to cancel in-flight requests so timed-out requests don't hold sockets
Verification plan: restart the job 5x in quick succession on the same TM pod and confirm FD count returns to baseline between restarts (does not step up)
Recommends a harness-level / unit test that loops open() and close() N times and asserts the FD count returned to baseline — catches the bug at PR time
Mentions a per-TM ref-counted channel registry (or static singleton with caveats) as a sophistication option to share one ManagedChannel across subtasks on the same TM
Starts with cross-cutting triage (FD trend over time, FD classification, top remotes via ss/lsof) — does NOT jump straight to a guessed aiohttp fix
Identifies the multi-remote socket-climb signature as a per-client construction leak (vs per-request body-not-closed), based on the 'many different remote services' clue
Recommends a single aiohttp.ClientSession per process, constructed in FastAPI `lifespan` (async context manager), and closed at shutdown
Provides an instrumentation snippet (e.g., monkey-patching `ClientSession.__init__`) to log construction stacks if the offending call site isn't obvious from grep
Recommends `py-spy dump --pid 1` to find coroutines parked in aiohttp request methods (which indicates missing timeouts masquerading as leaks)
Calls out per-request ClientSession construction as the dominant aiohttp leak in production code; doesn't suggest just adding more `async with` blocks at every call site without hoisting the session
Mentions `PYTHONTRACEMALLOC=10` (allocation traceback in warnings) and/or `-W error::ResourceWarning` (escalate ResourceWarning in CI) — the highest-leverage way to surface the offending call site
Mentions `enable_cleanup_closed=True` on `TCPConnector` (workaround for TLS sockets stuck in CLOSE_WAIT)
Diagnoses the root cause as the DB connection being held across the outbound Stripe HTTP call (transaction scope too wide), not just a missing close
